planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; peacock orchid (now classified as Gladiolus callianthus in current taxonomy) blooms in late summer and early autumn (August–October), producing white flowers with a dark maroon-purple blotch at the throat. The fragrance is powerfully sweet, particularly in the evening. In zones 7 and colder, lift corms after frost and store dry over winter; in zones 8–10 it may overwinter in the ground.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; peacock orchid is a beloved and exceptional corm — grown primarily for its combination of large, elegant white flowers and exceptional fragrance. In late summer and early autumn, the tall, narrow, sword-leaved stems carry on slender, arching stems a sequence of large (3-inch), pure-white, star-shaped flowers, each with a dramatic, well-defined, dark maroon-purple to blackish-purple blotch at the base of each petal that creates a bold, contrasting star pattern at the throat of each white flower. The flower form alone would be sufficient to recommend it — graceful, large, pure-white, star-shaped — but the fragrance transforms it into something exceptional: an intensely sweet, rich, spicy scent that intensifies in the evening and can fill a garden area with a perfume comparable to tuberose or Lilium regale in power and quality. Native to the Ethiopian highlands and East Africa, it blooms in late summer and early autumn (August–October) when most other fragrant bulbs have finished. Technically now classified as Gladiolus callianthus in current molecular taxonomy but still widely sold and grown under the Acidanthera name. Outstanding near seating areas and open windows where the evening fragrance can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant corms 3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; lurida moraea follows the Cape winter-rainfall growth cycle — emerging in autumn, blooming in spring (September–November in South Africa), going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. The flowers, which smell unpleasant to deter most insects but attract certain flies for pollination, are nonetheless visually striking. This is a rare species in cultivation — of botanical and collector interest.

directions: Plant corms in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; lurida moraea is a botanically unusual Cape iris — unusual primarily in two respects: the flower colors are in the warm brown-yellow-orange-cream range with contrasting dark spots and dark-patterned nectar guides (a lurid, dark-spotted quality that gives the species name 'lurida', meaning 'pale, sallow, discolored, lurid'), and the flowers emit a smell that, rather than attracting bees or butterflies, specifically attracts certain blowflies and carrion flies through a somewhat unpleasant odor — a pollination strategy of sapromyiophily (fly-pollination through deception). The flowers, however, are visually interesting and somewhat exotic-looking, with their warm, spotted, dark-centered color pattern. For the collector of unusual Cape irids, it occupies a different niche from the brilliant pure-blue geissorhizas or the vivid red-and-white gladioli — providing a curious, botanically interesting, darkly-patterned flower type that represents the diversity of pollination strategies evolved by Cape plants. A plant for botanical curiosity and collection rather than mass ornamental display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the upper half of the large, multi-layered bulb exposed above the soil surface in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Boophone disticha is extremely slow-growing — bulbs may take 10–20 years to reach flowering size. The bulb is highly toxic — one of the most toxic South African plants — containing alkaloids used by San Bushmen to poison arrows. The fan of stiff, wavy-edged leaves is arranged in a distinctive flat plane (disticha = two-ranked).

directions: Plant large bulbs (upper half exposed) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Boophone disticha is one of the most remarkable botanical curiosities of the South African flora — a plant of multiple botanical distinctions that make it a prize for collectors and botanically curious gardeners. The flat fan of stiff, wavy-margined, strap-shaped leaves arranged in a strict, two-ranked, fan-like plane (the disticha arrangement that gives the species name) is immediately distinctive and architecturally impressive. In spring, a dense, spherical to hemispherical umbel of 50–100 individual, bright-pink to deep-rose, tubular flowers appears on a stout scape from the large, grapefruit-to-soccer-ball-sized, multi-layered bulb — a vivid, spherical pompon of pink. After flowering, the entire, now-papery-dry seed head detaches from the scape and tumbles across the landscape like a tumbleweed, dispersing seeds by wind as it rolls — an extraordinary and unique seed-dispersal strategy for a bulb plant. The common name 'Sore-Eye Flower' refers to the traditional belief that its pollen causes irritation; the bulb is highly toxic and was used by indigenous peoples to poison arrowheads. The extraordinary combination of unique leaf arrangement, spectacular flower ball, tumbleweed seed dispersal, and legendary toxicity makes this one of the most interesting bulbs in the world. A patience plant — 10+ years to first flower from seed.

subcategory: slender South African pendulous wand flower

planting.note: Plant corms or established divisions in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; harebell wand flower is smaller and more slender than D. pulcherrimum — more suitable for smaller gardens and for placement in part shade where its more delicate form is appropriate. Like all dieramas, it resents disturbance and may sulk for 1–2 years after transplanting before flowering well. It is a coastal species, more adapted to humid conditions than D. pulcherrimum.

directions: Plant corms in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; harebell wand flower is the smaller, more slender, more coastal South African Dierama species — producing the same graceful, pendulous flowering effect as D. pulcherrimum (Angel's Fishing Rods) but on a smaller, more delicate scale: very fine, arching, wiry stems bearing a cascade of small, pendant, bell-shaped flowers in shades of pale pink to rose-pink to mauve-pink that hang on the thinnest of threadlike pedicels, swaying with hypnotic grace in any breeze. The smaller overall scale makes it more appropriate for smaller gardens and for positions in light dappled shade where D. pulcherrimum's greater stature and sun preference would be problematic. The individual bell flowers are slightly smaller and more numerous per stem than those of D. pulcherrimum, and the overall airy, pendulous effect of a colony in full bloom — scores of tiny pink bells hanging from dozens of swaying wiry stems — is arguably even more delicate and beautiful than the larger species. Native to the coastal cliffs, forest margins, and moist grasslands of the southeastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal, it is adapted to higher humidity than D. pulcherrimum, making it suitable for the humid zone 9 Gulf Coast conditions.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; Mobile onion is native to the moist, sandy, acidic coastal plain soils of Alabama, Mississippi, and western Florida, where it grows in open longleaf pine savannas, flatwoods, and sandy roadsides. It blooms in spring (March–April) with pink to deep-rose umbels. It prefers the moist, acidic sandy soils of the coastal plain rather than the alkaline limestone soils favored by A. drummond and A. kunthii.

directions: Plant small bulbs in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; Mobile onion is a Gulf Coast endemic allium — found natively in a relatively narrow band of the coastal plain from coastal Alabama through Mississippi and into the western Florida Panhandle — perfectly adapted to the moist, acidic, sandy soils of the longleaf pine flatwood ecosystems of this region. Unlike the limestone-adapted Texas wild onions (A. drummondii, A. kunthii) that favor dry, alkaline substrates, Mobile onion thrives in the opposite soil type: moist, humus-rich, acidic, sandy coastal plain soils of the piney woods and flatwood savannas. It produces in spring (March–April) attractive umbels of rose-pink to deep-pink, star-shaped flowers on stems 10–18 inches tall, providing a native spring pink in the understory of the coastal plain longleaf pine landscape. For gardeners in the Alabama-Mississippi Gulf Coast zone 9a area, this is the most locally native wild allium — a species naturally adapted to the exact soil chemistry, moisture, and light conditions of the local flatwood garden. It represents the native heritage of the specific coastal plain ecosystem of the central Gulf Coast.
